# Scenario 10: Circular Dependencies (What to Do When Your Dependency Graph Turns into a Cycle)

## What This Scenario Demonstrates
This scenario demonstrates how build tools handle cycles in the dependency graph (A -> B -> A).

## Dependency Graph
```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 4.4
graph LR
    Root[Project Root] --> LibA[lib-circle-a:1.0.0]
    LibA --> LibB[lib-circle-b:1.0.0]
    LibB --> LibA
```

### Maven (pom.xml)

```{.xml include="/demos/maven-demo/scenario-10-circular/pom.xml" snippet="s10-deps"}
```

## Expected Intuition
"Infinite loop! StackOverflowError!"

## Actual Resolution Results
*   **Maven**:
    *   **Build Time (Reactor)**: Fails immediately.
    *   **Resolution Time (Repo)**: Tolerates it by stopping recursion when it sees a visited node.
*   **Gradle**: Tolerates it.
*   **SBT**: Tolerates it.

## Classpath Reality
Both `lib-circle-a` and `lib-circle-b` end up on the classpath.

## Why This Happens
Build tools maintain a "visited" set during graph traversal to prevent infinite recursion.

## How Developers Commonly "Fix" This
Refactoring code to break the cycle (e.g., extracting a common interface to `lib-c`).

## Safer Ways to Take Control
Avoid cycles. They are a design smell.

## Signals to Watch For
*   `StackOverflowError` in some older tools or plugins.
*   Weird build order issues.

## How This Scales in Real Systems
Cycles make it impossible to build modules in a linear order, forcing them to be built together or released in lockstep.

## Key Takeaway
Circular dependencies are technically resolvable but architecturally disastrous.

## Related Scenarios
*   [Scenario 1: Basic](../scenario-1-basic/guide.qmd) - The ideal acyclic graph.
